skill.name: qutip
skill.description: Build and analyze quantum mechanics simulations with QuTiP, including states, operators, solvers, open-system dynamics, measurements, and visualization.
skill.summary: Use QuTiP references to model, solve, analyze, and visualize quantum systems in Python.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but review found they are false positives from Markdown code fences, quantum Hamiltonian notation, and QuTiP scientific APIs such as QFunc.eval. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, command execution logic, or malicious intent was found; residual risk is limited to package installation instructions and external documentation links.

low_findings.title: Benign Package Installation Instructions
low_findings.description: The skill includes uv pip install commands for QuTiP and optional related packages. This is expected for a scientific Python skill, but users should still install dependencies in a controlled environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are explicit installation commands, not hidden execution logic. The commands install named QuTiP packages and do not include shell chaining, remote scripts, or user-controlled arguments.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The skill links to QuTiP documentation, tutorials, API reference, and GitHub. These are normal reference links and no code in the skill sends data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only hardcoded URLs found are public QuTiP reference resources. No evidence found of network requests, credential handling, or telemetry.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Pattern Matches in Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: Most static findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, inline backticks, quantum Hamiltonian variable names, and scientific examples. No evidence found of prompt injection or malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of representative flagged locations shows documentation examples for QuTiP APIs. The suspicious tokens are not executable skill logic and do not create a command or code injection path.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Dynamic Code Evaluation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The reported eval pattern is qf.eval in a QuTiP QFunc example, which evaluates a quantum phase-space function over arrays. It is not JavaScript eval or Python eval on user-controlled text.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The context imports QFunc and calls qf.eval with numeric vectors. No string code input or dynamic execution primitive is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Dynamic Import Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The reported dynamic import location is a normal Python import statement split across lines for QuTiP superoperator utilities. It does not import a computed module name.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code imports named QuTiP symbols using static syntax. There is no importlib call, dynamic expression, or user-controlled module path.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The reported weak-cryptography hits are caused by quantum mechanics notation such as H for Hamiltonian, density matrices, and solver names. The skill does not implement cryptography.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited contexts are quantum simulation examples, not cryptographic algorithms. No hashing, cipher selection, or security protocol logic was found.

content.value_statement: Quantum simulation work requires precise state, operator, solver, and analysis choices. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused QuTiP guidance for building reliable Python simulations.
actual_capabilities: Explains QuTiP quantum objects, states, operators, tensor products, and common Hamiltonians.
Guides solver choice for closed systems, open systems, stochastic trajectories, and periodic Hamiltonians.
Shows analysis workflows for expectation values, entropy, fidelity, correlations, spectra, and steady states.
Provides visualization patterns for Bloch spheres, Wigner functions, Q-functions, Fock distributions, and matrix plots.
Covers advanced QuTiP topics including Floquet methods, HEOM, superoperators, and performance tuning.
limitations: It is documentation guidance and does not run simulations by itself.
Examples assume users can install QuTiP and related Python packages.
It does not verify physical model assumptions or experimental parameters.
Some advanced APIs may vary across QuTiP versions.

use_cases.title: Prototype Quantum Dynamics Models
use_cases.description: Create initial QuTiP models for Hamiltonians, collapse operators, time grids, and solver selection.

use_cases.title: Teach Quantum Simulation Concepts
use_cases.description: Generate clear examples that connect quantum states, operators, measurements, and visualization outputs.

use_cases.title: Debug Scientific Python Workflows
use_cases.description: Review QuTiP code for dimensional mismatches, solver choice, convergence checks, and plotting workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Simulation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the qutip skill to create a simple two-level quantum system simulation. Explain the state, Hamiltonian, time list, solver, and expected output.

prompt_templates.title: Model an Open System
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the qutip skill to model a damped harmonic oscillator with collapse operators. Include solver selection, observables, and convergence checks.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Entanglement Dynamics
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the qutip skill to design a two-qubit entanglement simulation. Track concurrence, explain decoherence terms, and describe useful plots.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Floquet Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the qutip skill to plan a Floquet analysis for a periodically driven qubit. Include assumptions, solver choices, outputs, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: Build a damped cavity simulation in QuTiP.
output_examples.output: A structured plan defining Hilbert space size, cavity Hamiltonian, decay collapse operator, mesolve usage, photon-number observable, and plotting steps.
A concise explanation of when to use each phase-space plot, what each plot shows, and how QuTiP computes the required arrays.

output_examples.input: Debug a slow Monte Carlo trajectory run.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering trajectory count, stored states, expectation operators, Hilbert-space truncation, tolerances, and parallel execution settings.

best_practices: Start with the smallest Hilbert space that captures the physics, then check convergence.
Choose solvers based on state type, dissipation, measurement model, and time dependence.
Track only needed observables when full state storage is not required.
anti_patterns: Using mesolve for every problem without checking whether a simpler solver is enough.
Increasing Hilbert space size without convergence tests or memory estimates.
Trusting plots without checking operator dimensions, units, and initial state normalization.

faq.question: What is QuTiP used for?
faq.answer: QuTiP is used to simulate quantum states, operators, dynamics, measurements, and visualizations in Python.

faq.question: Does this skill install QuTiP automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides installation commands and usage guidance, but the user controls any package installation.

faq.question: Can it help with open quantum systems?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for master equations, collapse operators, Monte Carlo trajectories, and dissipation models.

faq.question: Can it generate visualization workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Bloch spheres, Wigner functions, Q-functions, Fock distributions, and matrix visualizations.

faq.question: Is it suitable for quantum computing examples?
faq.answer: Yes, for state, gate, tensor-product, and entanglement examples. Some circuit features may require qutip-qip.

faq.question: What should users verify before trusting results?
faq.answer: Users should verify dimensions, solver assumptions, convergence, units, and whether truncation changes the result.
